In Judges 10, Israel finds itself caught in a familiar, grinding cycle—and this time the machinery of judgment grinds harder than before. Two judges, Tola and Jair, bring a period of stability and apparent prosperity. Jair's sons ride donkeys and hold thirty towns; there is wealth, order, relative peace. Then silence. No mention of these men calling Israel to faithfulness, no dramatic encounters with God. They simply judge for their allotted years and die. Into this quietness, Israel slips again into something worse than mere disobedience—they embrace a pantheon of foreign gods. Not one false god, but many: Baals, Ashtoreths, the deities of surrounding nations. They don't add to the LORD's worship; they forsake Him entirely. The catalog of idols is deliberate. The text wants you to see the absurdity of their betrayal: they turn from the God who has repeatedly saved them to gods with no power, no history with Israel, no stake in their survival.

God's response is sharp and deliberate. His anger burns, and He sells Israel into the hands of their enemies—not immediately to destruction, but to eighteen years of harassment and oppression. The Philistines and Ammonites press in from multiple directions, and "Israel was in deep distress." This is not abstract punishment; it is grinding, relentless suffering that breaks the people's will. Only then does their cry come, and notice what it is: acknowledgment of sin, not negotiation or excuse. "We have sinned against You, for we have indeed forsaken our God and served the Baals." But the LORD's response is devastating. He recounts His own faithfulness—the Egyptians, the Amorites, the Ammonites, the Philistines, all the enemies He has delivered Israel from—and then He withdraws the promise: "I will no longer save you. Go and cry out to the gods you have chosen."

These words should stop you cold. The LORD is telling Israel that mercy has limits, that continued betrayal can exhaust even divine patience. He is not threatening destruction; He is announcing abandonment. Let them learn whether their chosen gods can help them. Yet even here, something shifts. The Israelites respond not with despair but with complete surrender: "Deal with us as You see fit; but please deliver us today!" They put away their foreign gods and turn back to serving the LORD. And then comes the remarkable statement: "He could no longer bear the misery of Israel." The LORD's anger does not cool because Israel has earned it or because time has passed. His compassion—His inability to endure their suffering—becomes the reason for deliverance. This is not weakness in God; it is the nature of His character revealed in the midst of justified judgment. Even when threatened with abandonment, Israel's return moves Him to restore them.
